items.title: How an exercise-activated enzyme helps to keep the brain young
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00515-x A protein reinforces the blood–brain barrier, which becomes leaky with age.

items.title: How earthquakes and lightning help explain squeaky sneakers
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00619-4 High-speed footage reveals shoe squeaks can start with a tiny bolt of lightning — plus, evidence that a debated brain phenomenon exists in humans.

items.title: Limitations of probing field-induced response with STM
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10126-1 Limitations of probing field-induced response with STM

items.title: Cavity-altered superconductivity
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-025-10062-6 A new platform making use of hexagonal boron nitride interfaced with the molecular superconductor κ-(BEDT-TTF)2Cu[N(CN)2]Br is demonstrated for realizing cavity-altered materials, confirmed by magnetic force microscopy and nano-optical measurements.

items.title: Compact deep neural network models of the visual cortex
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10150-1 Parsimonious deep neural network models can be used for prediction of visual neuron responses.

items.title: Echinoderm stereom gradient structures enable mechanoelectrical perception
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10164-9 Sea urchin spines show exceptional mechanoelectrical sensing from gradient structures and can be replicated in 3D-printed mimics for underwater sensing.

items.title: A membrane-bound nuclease directly cleaves phage DNA during genome injection
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10207-1 A widespread bacterial defence system called SNIPE is shown to localize to the cell membrane, where it identifies and cleaves the DNA of infecting phage as it is injected into the bacterial cell.

items.title: Vectorized instructive signals in cortical dendrites
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10190-7 Mice learning a neurofeedback brain–computer interface task show neuron-specific teaching signals in cortical dendrites, consistent with a vectorized solution for credit assignment in the brain.

items.title: Peripheral immune-inducer dendritic cells drive early-life allergic inflammation
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10162-x Early-life exposure to allergens triggers a distinct local mode of dendritic cell activation in neonatal skin without requiring migration to lymph nodes, which shapes responses to allergens in later life.

items.title: Pancreatic-targeted lipid nanoparticles based on organ capsule filtration
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10158-7 Elucidation of fundamental pancreatic-targeted mechanisms enables the development of lipid nanoparticles for the precise delivery of nucleic acid therapeutics to the pancreas, highlighting their promising potential in the treatment of pancreatic diseases.

items.title: Squeaking at soft–rigid frictional interfaces
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10132-3 High-speed imaging reveals that the squeak of soft–rigid frictional interfaces, like sneakers sliding on a basketball court, arises from intersonic opening slip pulses—analogous to earthquake ruptures—that thin ridges on the rubber confine to repeat at a musical frequency.

items.title: Pivoting colloidal assemblies exhibit mechanical metamaterial behaviour
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10217-z A method is described for the manufacture of pivoting colloidal assemblies comprising rotating diamond and rotating triangle geometries that show tunable folding and unfolding by thermal fluctuations and actuation by magnetic attractions.

items.title: Clonal-aggregative multicellularity tuned by salinity in a choanoflagellate
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10137-y The choanoflagellate Choanoeca flexa forms motile and contractile cell monolayers purely clonally, purely aggregatively or through a combination of both processes depending on environmental conditions.

items.title: Convergent MurJ flippase inhibition by phage lysis proteins
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10163-w A common mechanism of inhibition of the essential lipid II flippase MurJ by three distinct phage-encoded single-gene lysis proteins provides insights into potential new targets for antimicrobial development.

items.title: OR7A10 GPCR engineering boosts CAR-NK therapy against solid tumours
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10149-8 The identification of ‘boosters’ that drive gene overexpression directly in a CAR construct provides a simple and scalable strategy for developing effective CAR-NK cell therapies for solid tumours.

items.title: A disease model resource reveals core principles of tissue-specific cancer evolution
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10187-2 The Mouse Cancer Cell line Atlas (MCCA) provides major advances towards a mechanistic understanding of cancer genomes.

items.title: Rewiring an E3 ligase enhances cold resilience and phosphate use in maize
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10142-1 The E3 ubiquitin ligase NLA postively regulates cold tolerance and negatively regulates phosphate uptake in maize, and a genetically engineered variant of this enzyme leads to improved cold tolerance and enhanced phosphate uptake, improving yield in field trials.

items.title: Field-free full switching of chiral antiferromagnetic order
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10175-6 An Mn3Sn homo-junction enables efficient field-free full switching of chiral antiferromagnets with greatly reduced power consumption, advancing their potential for energy-efficient magnetic memory devices with advantages of ultradense integration and ultrafast speed.

items.title: Hydrofluorocarbon electrolytes for energy-dense and low-temperature batteries
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10210-6 A method using hydrofluorocarbon electrolytes for synthesizing alkanes with monofluorinated structures is described, yielding a pathway for manufacturing lithium-metal batteries able to achieve high energy density as well as operate at low temperature.

items.title: Argentine fossil rewrites evolutionary history of a baffling dinosaur clade
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10194-3 A skeleton of the alvarezsauroid Alnashetri cerropoliciensis—representing a highly complete alvarezsauroid skeleton from South America—provides evidence on the evolution of the peculiar anatomy and miniaturization within this unusual theropod dinosaur clade.

items.title: Coral microbiomes as reservoirs of unknown genomic and biosynthetic diversity
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10159-6 Reconstructing microbial genomes from 820 reef-building corals collected at 99 reefs across 32 islands throughout the Pacific Ocean highlights the importance of conserving coral reefs as vital reservoirs of molecular diversity.

items.title: CLCC1 promotes hepatic neutral lipid flux and nuclear pore complex assembly
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-025-10064-4 CRISPR–Cas9 screening identifies CLCC1 as a factor that increases neutral lipid flux to prevent hepatic steatosis and promotes nuclear pore complex assembly by promoting membrane bending and fusion.

items.title: CLCC1 governs ER bilayer equilibration to maintain lipid homeostasis
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10161-y Phospholipid scrambling at the endoplasmic reticulum is essential for establishing a dynamic equilibrium to maintain cellular homeostasis.

items.title: Functional dissection of complex trait variants at single-nucleotide resolution
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10121-6 Massively parallel reporter assays across five cell types identify thousands of causal, noncoding regulatory variants among 220,000 loci, revealing diverse regulatory mechanisms shaping complex traits and disease.

items.title: Uncovering origins of heterogeneous superconductivity in La<sub>3</sub>Ni<sub>2</sub>O<sub>7</sub>
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-025-10095-x Wide-field quantum sensing shows μm-scale inhomogeneous superconductivity in high-pressure La3Ni2O7, linking local diamagnetic response to stress and stoichiometry and clarifying mechanisms that suppress or enhance superconductivity.

items.title: Human hippocampal neurogenesis in adulthood, ageing and Alzheimer’s disease
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10169-4 Mapping of neurogenesis in human hippocampi across ages and different cognitive abilities using multiomic single-cell sequencing reveals distinct signatures between cognitive preservation and decline.

items.title: Entanglement-assisted non-local optical interferometry in a quantum network
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10171-w Entangled quantum memories are used in a quantum network of silicon–vacancy centres in diamond nanocavities to experimentally perform non-local phase measurements.

items.title: EU leaders should not rush to revamp green-hydrogen rules
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00560-6 Some policymakers think that climate-friendly policies are impeding the adoption of green hydrogen. They should ask scientists to review the evidence.

items.title: Stem cells provide a potent treatment for frailty
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00584-y Elderly people with frailty, which affects up to one-quarter of over-50s, increased their endurance after a single dose of stem cells.

items.title: We need a global assessment of avoidable climate-change risks
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00544-6 To understand the urgency of emissions reductions, policymakers and citizens need a full analysis of what is at stake.

items.title: The age of animal experiments is waning. Where will science go next?
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00563-3 Advances in organ and computer models are raising the prospect that some animal experiments could be eliminated. But there are still huge hurdles to overcome.

items.title: The protein carriers of hundreds of lipids have been identified
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00570-4 Different types of lipid molecule are transported around the cell by lipid-transfer proteins. Biochemical approaches have been adapted to measure which lipids are captured by the more than 130 known human lipid-transfer proteins, providing a valuable resource that reveals some principles of how these transporters work.

items.title: Flexible paths to multicellularity
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00292-7 Close relatives of animals can become multicellular if distinct individuals join together or if dividing cells remain attached. A species has been found to use both mechanisms.

items.title: Brains of ‘super agers’ are strong producers of new neurons
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00599-5 Older people with exceptional memory have a surprisingly high number of young neurons, study finds.

items.title: Environmental exposure trains the immune system to dampen allergic responses
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00040-x Clean living conditions that lessen exposure to microorganisms are linked to an increase in allergies. Mouse data reveal how the environment affects allergic immune responses.

items.title: Scientists face fallout for past associations with Epstein
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00554-4 Jack Horner, who was a consultant for the Jurassic Park films, is among the researchers whose work or careers have been affected because of revelations from newly released documents.

items.title: Parkinson’s disease affects network of brain regions that controls whole-body action
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00573-1 Precision brain imaging of individuals with Parkinson’s disease reveals that the affected circuit is best characterized as a previously described network of brain regions called the somato-cognitive action network. Abnormally high connectivity in this network is associated with Parkinson’s disease and is normalized by effective pharmacological treatment and various brain-stimulation therapies.

items.title: The secret of squeaky basketball shoes
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00295-4 When shoes slide across a floor, wave-like deformations of the sole can generate squeaking. The pitch of the squeak depends on the rate at which deformations are generated.

items.title: The future perfect continuous passive and other transitive disorders of the mind
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00132-8 Grammar is destiny.

items.title: The first ice-core record of historical atmospheric hydrogen levels
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00578-w An ice-core record of hydrogen reveals that atmospheric levels vary in response to climate change and have increased drastically owing to human activity since the pre-industrial era. The apparent sensitivity of hydrogen cycling to environmental conditions should be considered when projecting the consequences of rising anthropogenic hydrogen emissions in a warming climate.

items.title: Amplified X-ray laser pulses achieved using mirror set-up
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00580-2 Current X-ray lasers require facilities that are kilometres long. A cavity-based set-up about 66 metres in length has been developed that uses highly reflective mirrors to enable the generation of high-intensity pulses of narrow-band X-ray radiation. This promises to overcome the limitations of current X-ray lasers.

items.title: What's the best way to change research fields? These three scientists have ideas
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-025-04161-7 Planning to switch fields? Be bold, know your limits and choose your institution wisely, say three UK research leaders.

items.title: Pop-up journals for policy research: can temporary titles deliver answers?
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00446-7 Journals that focus on specific research questions could help to bridge the science–policy gap, if they can attract researchers.

items.title: Five ways to spot when a paper is a fraud
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00569-x Science sleuths share their common-sense tips for sniffing out fishy articles.

items.title: Protein engineering fixes a major crop trade-off
items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00293-6 Rational design of a regulatory protein decouples the ability to tolerate cold from the means to acquire phosphorus, effectively enhancing crop yield under cold stress.

items.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00374-6 The spines of sea urchins can generate a voltage when water moves around them — a phenomenon that could be used to design underwater flow sensors.

items.title: A wafer-scale optoelectronic device unlocks monolithic 3D integration
items.summary: Nature Nanotechnology, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41565-026-02127-y Ordered vacancies in boridene introduce anomalous electrical anisotropy, enabling bipolar and linear photocurrent for optoelectronic computing. items.title: Runaway electrons during a coil quench in stellarators
authors: Pavel Aleynikov, Per Helander, and Håkan M. Smith
items.summary: The stellarator concept for future fusion reactors has a key advantage over the tokamak, being practically immune to large-scale disruptions. The authors show, however, that a rapid shutdown of stellarator coil currents (with fast dissipation of poloidal magnetic flux) can nonetheless drive an avalanche of runaway electrons, even without any interruption of the net toroidal plasma current. The problem is far less serious than in a tokamak, but some runaways are inevitably present in an activated fusion device, so an accidental rapid coil ramp-down could produce a dangerous runaway current. Some form of dedicated intervention is likely necessary.

items.summary: Nature Physics, Published online: 20 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-025-03156-5 In a Bose–Einstein condensate, bosonic stimulation enhances light scattering. An experiment now reveals that interatomic interactions diminish this effect, offering a probe of quantum correlations.

An experiment now shows that attractive atomic interactions enhance this scattering further, while repulsive ones can completely suppress bosonic stimulation.

authors: Fukuan Wang, Chen Zhou, Busheng Wang, and Yong Liu
items.summary: Fast transport of Na + limits the rate capability of next-generation sodium-ion batteries, and remains a longstanding challenge for high-energy cathode materials. Using an ab initio evolutionary search combined with first-principles calculations, this study identifies two metastable NaMnO 2 polymorphs that host unusually open Na coordination environments, stabilized by high-pressure synthesis. These phases exhibit extremely low Na + migration barriers, while maintaining competitive operating voltages and robust structures during (de)sodiation. The results highlight metastability as a powerful design principle for fast-ion-conducting cathodes beyond conventional layered frameworks.

items.summary: Probing the interface of topological insulators and ferromagnets has proven persistently challenging, despite its relevance for spintronic applications such as spin-charge interconversion. This work provides a powerful methodology to separate bulk and interfacial spin processes based on their different dynamics. The authors optically inject femtosecond spin currents from a ferromagnetic metal into an adjacent thin film of the topological insulator Bi 2 Te 3 . The twofold dynamics of the resulting femtosecond charge current contain signatures of spin-charge interconversion by the bulk inverse spin Hall effect and the interfacial inverse Edelstein effect.

items.title: Low-crosstalk silicon-fabricated optical waveguides for laser delivery to matter qubits
items.summary: Craft et al. Efficient, precise control of trapped-ion qubits is essential to scaling up quantum computing technology using that platform, and one approach utilizes integrated photonic waveguides to individually address the qubits. However, crosstalk is typically mitigated by spacing the waveguides far beyond the scale of the qubits, which hinders mode matching and efficiency. The authors identify relatively simple and easily implemented design choices that yield low crosstalk with such systems while keeping the waveguide pitch close to the qubit pitch, to facilitate light delivery and collection.

items.summary: Global quantum networking will be needed for quantum secured communication, and for linking distant quantum computers. Its terrestrial realization is held back by exponential photon loss in optical fibers, though, so we turn to space. The authors propose a quantum repeater architecture based on low-earth-orbit satellites that transmit entangled photons to single-atom quantum memories. They quantify the system’s expected performance, in terms of entanglement distribution rates and fidelities, and suggest a multiplexing approach to enable entanglement creation across distances of 10,000–20,000 km, bringing a global quantum Internet within reach.
